Consent to Participate in Research
Do not sign this consent if today's date is later than the stated expiration date above.

Title of Research Study: Alzheimer's Disease Research Center, STU00023196

Principal Investigator: Gary Rosenberg, MD

Supported By: The National Institute on Aging

If your doctor is also the person responsible for this research study, please note that they are interested in both your clinical care and the conduct of this research study. You have the right to discuss this study with another person who is not part of the research team before deciding whether to participate in the research.

Key Information:
The first few pages of this document include a summary of this study to help you decide whether or not to participate. Detailed information is provided after the summary.

WHAT IS THE PURPOSE, PROCEDURES, AND DURATION OF THE STUDY?

By doing this study, we hope to learn about biological information that is related to Alzheimer's Disease, and other related disorders.  Your participation in this research will last about 10 hours the first year. We would like to see you every year after that for a visit if you are still interested and able. Visits after the first year will be about 4-6 hours.

WHAT ARE THE KEY REASONS YOU MIGHT CHOOSE TO VOLUNTEER FOR THIS STUDY?

There may be no benefit to you. The information we learn may help to expand our understanding of cognitive disorders. It is possible you may learn information about your own health in this study. For a complete description of benefits, refer to the Detailed Consent.

WHAT ARE THE KEY REASONS YOU MIGHT NOT CHOOSE TO VOLUNTEER FOR THIS STUDY?

The research will take many hours. It is possible you will not learn anything further about your health. Although the procedure risks are minimal, they may occur. For a complete description of the risks, refer to the Detailed Consent/Appendix. There are no alternatives other than not to participate.

DO YOU HAVE TO TAKE PART IN THE STUDY?

If you decide to take part in the study, it should be because you really want to volunteer. You will not lose any services, benefits or rights you would normally have if you choose not to volunteer.

WHAT IF YOU HAVE QUESTIONS, SUGGESTIONS OR CONCERNS?

The person in charge of this study is Dr. Gary Rosenberg of the University of New Mexico Health Sciences Center, Center for Memory & Aging. If you have questions, suggestions, or concerns regarding this study or you want to withdraw from the study, you should contact 505-272-5631 or 505-272-7345.

If you have any questions or concerns about your rights as a volunteer in this research, contact staff in the University of New Mexico Health Sciences (UNMHSC) Human Research Review Committee (HRRC) between the business hours of 8AM and 5PM, Mountain Standard Time (MST), Monday-Friday at 505-272-1129.

Detailed Information:
The rest of this document includes detailed information about this study (in addition to the information listed above).

ARE THERE REASONS WHY YOU WOULD NOT QUALIFY FOR THIS STUDY?

You may not be able to participate if you are unable to get an MRI or if you are unable to complete the cognitive testing due to cognitive decline or severe visual impairment. Also, if you have other exclusionary diseases, such as schizophrenia, multiple sclerosis, or active drug or alcohol abuse, you may not be able to participate.

WHERE IS THE STUDY GOING TO TAKE PLACE AND HOW LONG WILL IT LAST?

The research procedures will be conducted at Center for Memory & Aging research offices.    The total amount of time you will be asked to volunteer for this study is up to 10 hours during the first year. Scheduling of visits will be dependent on each participant's availability. All follow-up years will be about 4-6 hours of time. Some procedures may be conducted by phone or computer if you prefer. The MRI, blood and spinal fluid collection, and medical exams are required to be completed in person.

WHAT WILL YOU BE ASKED TO DO?

If you agree to be in this study, you will be asked to read and sign this Consent Form. Informed consent process may be conducted virtually, over telephone or in person as allowed by UNM Human Research Protection Office due to COVID-19 related restrictions or based on your own preferences. After the consent information is reviewed, a series of questions will be asked to ensure you understand the research.

Some of the following questionnaires, medical history and cognitive testing may be performed by telephone or video to reduce face-to-face time during COVID restrictions.

Medical Information/exam

Your personal and family medical history will be reviewed. If you agree to get contrast during the MRI, additional questions about your history of kidney function or disease and liver transplant history will be done. During the exam the following information will be collected: height, weight, blood pressure, pulse, nervous system function (neurological exam). You will be asked about your current medications. You will also be asked to complete a measure of balance and gait. This will measure how steady you are on your feet.

Previous Research Data

If you have participated in previous research studies associated with UNM Center for Memory and Aging, we request to share that information, MRI images, and data already collected into this research study's database as well as with NIH databases. The old data will be used to compare to the new data we collect. You can choose Yes or No at the end of the consent.

Questionnaires

You will be asked to complete a questionnaire that asks you about your feelings (such as "Do you feel sad?") as well as questions about your behavior and functioning. You may also be asked to complete questionnaires related to COVID-19 impact on your life and your technological capability to do remote assessments.

Study partner

We ask all participants to identify a study partner, if possible. A study partner might be a spouse, family member, friend or neighbor who you see regularly. You are able to participate without a study partner, however, the addition of information from someone who lives with you or sees you frequently is helpful. There is a separate optional agreement for the study partner to complete as an appendix at the end of this consent.

If you have a study partner, they will be asked to complete demographics about themselves as well as to answer some questionnaires about your memory, behavior, and daily functioning. Your study partner may also be asked other questions about your medical or family history if you are not sure or unable to do so.

Cognitive tests 

You will be administered a test of cognitive functioning to determine the type of trouble that you are having with your thinking. This will take about one to two hours.  During some of the cognitive tests, we will be audio recording your responses to ensure accurate scoring.  The audio recording will be deleted after transcription.

MRI 

The magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan will be performed at the Mind Research Network (MRN) which is in the same building as the Center for Memory & Aging. Some people cannot have a MRI test because, for example, they have a heart pacemaker. If you are pregnant or may be pregnant, you cannot get an MRI for research.  If there is a question of pregnancy, you will be asked to take a urine pregnancy test prior to the MRI.

During the MRI, you may have an IV and a contrast agent will be injected into a vein to study your brain blood vessels. This type of scan may help to give more information to the study doctors about the blood vessels in your brain. If your kidney function tests are abnormal, you will not receive contrast. This is an optional test that you can choose. This may be done at a second MRI. If you are asked to do any additional MRIs, you will be compensated for the additional MRI as well.

The MRI will take pictures of your brain. If the MRI results are unclear, you may be asked to have an additional MRI done. For this study, you will lie down on a table and will then be placed into a long donut-shaped magnet. During the study you will hear loud rapping and knocking noises coming from the magnet. You will be given headphones to wear to protect your hearing. You may also listen to music during the MRI if you would like. You may feel warm during this procedure. In order to obtain good pictures, it is important that you do not move during the procedure. Although you should not talk while the MRI is taking pictures, you will be able to talk with the technician during breaks or in case of emergency by pressing a call button or similar device. This MRI will last for approximately 1 to 1 ½ hours. You will be asked to change into medical scrubs, complete an MRI safety screening form, and remove all metal prior to the MRI. The total time needed for the MRI may be 2 to 3 hours.

The MRI scan is being done to answer research questions, not to examine your brain for medical reasons. This MRI scan is not a substitute for a clinical scan (the type a doctor would order).  The research scan might not show problems that may be picked up by a clinical MRI scan.  However, all research MRI scans will be read by a neuroradiologist (a doctor with experience reading MRI scans) unless you have been scanned at MRN in the previous six months.  If we find an abnormality that requires follow-up, we may call you or mail a copy of the report to you, or contact you and your doctor (with your permission) by phone to help answer questions.

Lumbar Puncture

A lumbar puncture is sometimes called a spinal tap. Some people cannot have this test because, for example, they are on blood thinning medication. A medical doctor will perform the test, involving removal of spinal fluid through a needle. You will be asked to lay down or sit leaning over an exam table for this procedure.  This test will take about 60 minutes.

The lumbar puncture will be done when you are fasting (have not eaten in the last 8-12 hours), so is typically scheduled for morning.

Research Blood Tests

During the study, a blood sample of about 20 teaspoons of blood will be taken from a vein in your arm.  This will be done with a needle. The reason for taking your blood is to compare the chemicals found in your spinal fluid to those found in your blood. Blood tests will also be done to check for any inflammatory markers that might be important in Alzheimer's Disease and related disorders. 

Biological Sample and Data Sharing

The blood and CSF samples will have labels bearing your study number and the date it was taken. Some samples will be stored in Dr. Rosenberg's laboratory in freezers for future analysis. Your biosamples and data may also be used for research on other conditions; for example, as comparisons to other diseases. This could include a wide variety of conditions such as Alzheimer's disease, other forms of dementia, mental illness, HIV/AIDS, cancer, heart disease, and others. Because we cannot predict all the research questions and projects that may benefit from your samples and other data, there is no expressed or implied limit to the use of your biosamples and other data for research purposes.

Some biological samples will be sent to the National Centralized Repository for Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias (NCRAD) for sharing with other investigators. NCRAD is a national resource supported by the National Institute on Aging (NIA) that prepares and stores biological specimens from all over the world and makes them available to approved scientists who would not otherwise be able to access this material. Your biological samples will be maintained in the laboratory indefinitely. These samples will be coded and not identified by your name. The samples are necessary for long-term research and will be stored indefinitely. The biological samples allow for studies of the causes of many diseases, including Alzheimer's disease or other related dementias.

MRI images and other data collected as part of this study will be shared with National Institute on Aging and other NIH data repositories.  All data are shared without any identifying information. Biological samples and data will be shared with approved researchers.  The biological samples may be provided to researchers at academic institutions, hospitals, and private companies. Specimens collected from you for this research may be used to develop products which could be sold in the future. The researchers do not plan to share any profits or losses from the sale of those products with you.

Mind Research Network Data Sharing

We would also like to request your permission to store all of the data that was collected in this study in The Mind Research Network Data Sharing Repository for other, future research. The stored data will include information such as your age and gender, as well as assessment data that were collected about you during the course of this study. It is possible that this information may remain linked to your name. It will be handled with the same care and confidentially as it is for the current study. You have the right to withdraw your permission to share your data for research purposes at any time following your participation in this study. To do so, please contact the Principal Investigator directly. You will have the option to choose at the end of this document.

Genetic Tests

It is possible that genetic tests may be done with your sample to determine Alzheimer's and related disorders related genetic markers. Your biological samples collected as part of this study may be used for genomic analyses such as whole genome sequencing. Whole genome sequencing involves determining the exact order of the base pairs (chemical letters) of your DNA. Other genomic technologies may be used and continue to be developed. If whole genome sequencing is done, it will be coded and we will not be able to match you to this information.

Standard blood tests

If you have not had any recent laboratory tests done by your family doctor, we may ask you to complete tests for blood sugar, blood cell count, cholesterol or kidney function. We will ask you to get copies of blood tests that were completed in the last year so they are not repeated.

If you will be getting contrast in the MRI, blood tests must be completed to check your kidney functioning before the MRI.

A blood sample of about 2-4 teaspoons of blood will be taken from a vein in your arm. This will be done with a needle. This may be done at a Tricore location or at Memory and Aging offices.

Yearly Follow-up Visits

We would like to see all participants back for a study visit yearly until you are no longer able or willing to come back. At every follow-up visit, you will complete the same questionnaires, medical exams, cognitive testing and blood tests. Every other year, you will have another MRI unless changes in your medical conditions prevent this. The collection of spinal fluid is only done at the first year.

The number of years we are able to follow participants will depend on renewed funding, but may be 10 or more years.

If you are no longer able to come in to research visits due to a move, medical conditions, or other situation, we would like to check in by phone regarding your current medical and cognitive functioning. If at any time you are unable to relay this information, we would like your permission to talk to a member of your family about your current health. This may be your study partner or other family member or friend. We will ask you who should be contacted if we are unable to get in touch with you at any time in the future. You can agree or disagree at the end of this document. You can also change your mind or modify your contact person at any time during the study by letting us know at your next study visit or by notifying us in writing at:

Center for Memory & Aging

MSC 11 6035

1 University of New Mexico

Albuquerque, NM 87131-0001

 

WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS AND DISCOMFORTS?

Cognitive Testing and Questionnaires

The testing of mental functions presents practically no risk to you.  However, these tests can sometimes produce boredom and fatigue.  The time it takes to complete testing may take you away from other things you'd rather be doing. Some of the questionnaires may ask questions that may be sensitive or make you feel uncomfortable. 

Audio recordings of your responses may involve a slightly greater risk of loss of confidentiality than purely paper-based information; however, we will only identify the recording by study ID number and will delete once the recording has been transcribed.

Lumbar Puncture (LP)

The lumbar puncture may cause pain at the site where the needle goes in and the spinal fluid is taken. This will be minimized by giving the skin an injection of medicine to numb the area.  Lidocaine will be used to numb your back. Please inform the investigator if you are allergic to Lidocaine.

There is a small risk of infection or bleeding.  Risks of bleeding will be minimized by not doing a lumbar puncture on people who take blood thinners or have other problems forming blood clots.  If you are on warfarin, dabigatran, rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban or other Low Molecular Weight heparins you will not be able to receive a lumbar puncture. If you have had lower back surgery or have significant back issues, you may not be a candidate for having the optional lumbar puncture.

An infection could spread throughout the nervous system.  Risk of infection will be minimized by cleaning your skin very carefully.  Although these are serious problems of lumbar puncture they are so rare that there is no information about how often they occur. 

After the lumbar puncture you may get a headache.  About a third of adults report a headache after an LP. This headache is different from other headaches because it will go away when you lay down and come back when sitting or standing. If you have this kind of headache and it does not go away after 48 hours, call one of the research staff.   To minimize the risk of a headache, the doctor will use a small needle and may prescribe bed rest for one or more hours after the procedure.  If a headache occurs, it is usually mild and can be controlled by bed rest, drinking lots of fluids and a pain pill, such as acetaminophen.  Rarely, the headache is severe and may require additional treatment with a "blood patch."  In this procedure, a small amount of your own blood is injected into the lumbar puncture site.  This procedure is generally effective in stopping the headache. 

To minimize these risks, the lumbar puncture procedure will be performed by a medical professional specifically trained to do this procedure.

The amount of spinal fluid that is drawn is minimal (4 teaspoons). The spinal fluid that we take is replaced by your body in a matter of hours. 

Blood Drawing

The risks of drawing your blood include soreness, bruising, pain, infection, possible fainting, and bleeding.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging

The risks of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are minimal and no different than risks with MRI scanning for other purposes.  The most common problem is claustrophobia and this occurs in about 5% of people.  Claustrophobia is an uncomfortable feeling of anxiety about being inside the magnet tube.  If this happens, you will be removed immediately from the scanner and uncomfortable feelings will resolve promptly.  The MRI machine makes loud noises such as knocking or buzzing during its operation.  You will be offered headphones to protect your hearing and make the experience more pleasant. You can also listen to music during the test.  Less often, people experience some mild dizziness when first standing up after they get out of the magnet tube after lying down for over an hour.  This passes quickly and technicians will make certain you can sit for a brief period until you are steady to walk out of the scan area.  The most serious problem relates to metal objects (such as a heart pacemaker) in or around your body.  The MRI's strong magnet can affect these metal objects; if these are present, you will not be able to have the scan.  Technicians will assure there are no reasons for concern by asking you a series of questions before you can enter the scanner. There is a very small danger that a metal object will be released in the room during the study.  That object could become a projectile and injure you.  Every effort will be made to assure that no metal objects are present during your study.

 No long-term harmful effects from MRI are known. However, since the effect of MRI on early development of the fetus is unknown, participants who are pregnant should not go in the MRI.  Rarely, large tattoos can heat up during an MRI scan and cause skin irritation like a sunburn, so the MRI technologist will want to see any tattoos you have prior to the scan.  

 Due to the very high sensitivity of MRI in detecting abnormalities, there is a risk of false-positive findings, identifying something on imaging studies that may or may not be important.  This may result in anxiety and additional testing, possibly including a recommendation for clinical scans at your cost.  The radiology report or other study data will not be put into your medical record unless you provide it to your physician.  If the radiology report becomes part of your personal medical record, it may or may not have an effect on getting health insurance or life insurance in the future. Radio and magnetic waves associated with MRI examinations are not associated with any known adverse effects.

Gadolinium (contrast MRI)

Gadolinium, the contrast given during a MRI, will be given by injection into a vein in your arm. This may cause some minor pain, and may cause some bruising near the area of the injection. The gadolinium may also cause the following: headache, nausea, and vomiting. Rarely, it may cause dizziness, rash, itching, numbness or a tingling feeling in the hands or feet (less than 10% chance). Allergic reactions to gadolinium are very rare and occur in less than 1 out of 10,000 of people. If you have an allergic reaction during the MRI, there is a doctor available during the MRI to help you.

Persons with severe kidney disease or liver transplants may develop a rare debilitating condition called Nephrogenic Systemic Fibrosis after they receive gadolinium. This disorder affects the skin, and may involve the lungs, liver, muscles and heart. Therefore, if you have kidney disease you will not be allowed to get the contrast. Precautions are taken to prevent all of the above, and the risks are considered minimal. Your kidney function (eGFR) will be checked by a blood test prior to administration of contrast.

It has been recognized that repeated MRI scans with contrast agent Gadolinium may result in residual Gadolinium remaining in the brain but no adverse effects have been associated with this to date. It is currently unclear and controversial if this has any long-term effects, but it is possible that this could present a possible adverse effect in the future.

Genetic testing risks

Even without your name or identifiers, genetic information is unique to you making it possible for someone to trace it back to you. The results of genetic research apply to both you and your family members. In some cases, it could be used to make it harder for you to get or keep a job or insurance. Genetic information could be used in ways that could cause you or your family distress.

There is a federal law called the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA). Generally, GINA makes it illegal for health insurance companies, group health plans, and most employers to discriminate against you based on your genetic information. Be aware that GINA does not protect you against discrimination by companies that sell life insurance, disability insurance, or long-term care insurance. It also does not prohibit discrimination on the basis of already known genetic disease. 

If Whole Genome Sequencing is done, your sample will be deidentified and the data will not be able to be linked back to you. 

Other Risks

As with all research, there is always a possibility of a breach of confidentiality.

There is always a chance that any medical treatment can harm you. The research procedures in this study are no different. In addition to risks described in this consent, you may experience a previously unknown risk or side effect.

WILL YOU BENEFIT FROM TAKING PART IN THIS STUDY?

We do not know if you will get any benefit from participating in this study. However, some people have learned more about their own health by participating. If you take part in this study, information learned may help people with Alzheimer's disease and related dementias.

WHAT WILL IT COST YOU TO PARTICIPATE?

There are no costs associated with taking part in the study.

WHO WILL SEE THE INFORMATION THAT YOU GIVE?

When we write about or share the results from the study, we will write about the combined information. We will keep your name and other identifying information private.

We will make every effort to prevent anyone who is not on the research team from knowing that you gave information, or what the information is. You will be given a unique study identification number. This number and your initials will be used to record your study information, MRI pictures, and blood and spinal fluid samples. A log of the participant names, participant ID numbers, and personal information (such as home address, telephone number, and emergency contact information) will be maintained in a locked area at the clinical site. Only authorized members of the research study will have permission to see this data.

The data collected in this study will be stored for 6 years after the study has been closed with the IRB.

All of the information we collect about you will be coded with a unique research subject identifier (URSI) or other subject code and will be kept on password protected computers, and stored securely in restricted and protected databases according to MRN and UNMHSC information security policies.

Your MRI pictures will be stored and at the end of the study, the record linking your name and other identifying information to your URSI (which the study data is labeled with) will be made unavailable to the research team; however, it will be kept indefinitely (forever) at the MRN in a confidential manner so that you may continue to have access to your MRI information.

You should know there are some circumstances in which we may have to show your information to other people. For example, the law may require us to share your information with the following agencies and for the following reasons:

  • A court or agencies, if you have a reportable disease or condition.
  • Authorities, if you report information about a child being abused, or if you pose a danger to yourself or someone else.

A Certificate of Confidentiality from the National Institutes of Health covers this research. The researchers with this Certificate of Confidentiality may not disclose or use the information, documents, or biospecimens that may identify you in any federal, state, or local civil, criminal, administrative, legislative, or other action, suit or proceeding, or be used as evidence, for example, if there is a court subpoena, unless you have consented for this use. Information, documents, or biospecimens protected by the Certificate of Confidentiality cannot be disclosed to anyone who is not connected to the research. Except if, there is a federal, state, or local law that requires disclosure, (such as to report child abuse or communicable diseases but not for federal, state, or local civil, criminal, administrative, legislative or other proceedings); if you have consented to the disclosure, including for your medical treatment, or if it is used for other scientific research, as allowed by federal regulations protecting research subjects.

CAN YOU CHOOSE TO WITHDRAW FROM THE STUDY EARLY?

You can choose to leave the study at any time. You will not be treated differently if you decide to stop taking part in the study.

If you choose to leave the study early, data collected until that point will remain in the study database and may not be removed.  Data and samples that have already been distributed to approved researchers will not be retrieved.

If you experience changes which prevent you from completing the entire yearly follow-up, we may ask you to complete any partial tests or information that you are able to complete. 

If you choose to withdraw from the active study, you may be asked to maintain contact to determine how you are doing clinically. We will ask if we can still contact you yearly to check-in. 

ARE YOU PARTICIPATING, OR CAN YOU PARTICIPATE, IN ANOTHER RESEARCH STUDY AT THE SAME TIME AS PARTICIPATING IN THIS ONE?

You may take part in this study if you are currently involved in another research study. It is important to let the investigator know if you are in another research study. You should discuss this with the investigator before you agree to participate in another research study while you are in this study.

WHAT HAPPENS IF YOU GET HURT OR SICK DURING THE STUDY?

If you believe you are hurt or if you get sick because of something that is due to the study, you should call the study investigators at 505-272-7456 or 505-272-5631 immediately.

It is important for you to understand that the University of New Mexico does not have funds set aside to pay for the cost of any care or treatment that might be necessary because you get hurt or sick while taking part in this study. Also, the University of New Mexico will not pay for any wages you may lose if you are harmed by this study.

Medical costs related to your care and treatment because of study-related harm will be sought from your insurer, managed care plan, or other benefits program. If you do not have insurance, you may be responsible for these costs. You will also be responsible for any associated co-payments or deductibles required by your insurance.

WILL I BE PAID FOR PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?

Key Information:
The first few pages of this document include a summary of this study to help you decide whether or not to participate. Detailed information is provided after the summary.

You are invited to take part in a study about memory and aging, including Alzheimer's Disease and Related disorders.

WHY IS THIS STUDY BEING DONE?
We hope to learn about memory and aging. Your participation in this study may include the following things: signing a consent form, collection of personal and medical information, evaluating your thinking, collection of blood samples for biomarkers, and optional radiographic brain pictures. If you complete a brain picture, you will receive the results. The results can be shared with your doctor. Detailed descriptions of all of the study activities are in the attached form. We would like to do all the above listed procedures again every year for as long as you would like to. The study may take up to up to 10 hours each year. If you need to travel, this would include additional travel time and compensation.

WHY MIGHT YOU WANT TO DO THIS?
This study may help scientists come up with new ways to help others who have noticed changes in their thinking (detailed below).

WHY MIGHT YOU NOT WANT TO DO THIS?
The study will take several hours. You may not learn anything more about your health. For all of the risks, read the attached form.

DO YOU HAVE TO DO THIS STUDY?
No. If you decide to be in the study, it should be because you really want to. You will not lose any rights you would normally have if you decide not to be in the study.

WHAT IF YOU HAVE QUESTIONS OR WORRIES?
The person in charge of this study is Dr. Gary Rosenberg. He is a doctor at the University of New Mexico. If you have questions about this study or you want to stop being in the study, please call 505-272-3315.

If you have any questions or concerns about being in this study, call the University of New Mexico Health Sciences Human Research Review Committee. You would need to call between business hours of 8AM and 5PM, Monday-Friday at 505-272-1129.

Detailed Information:
The rest of this document includes detailed information about this study (in addition to the information listed above).

ARE THERE REASONS WHY YOU MAY NOT BE ABLE TO DO THIS STUDY?
There are three main reasons why you may not be able to be in this study. 1) If you are not able
to sign the consent form. 2) If you are not able to do the tests of thinking. 3) You have other
diseases such as schizophrenia or multiple sclerosis, 4) If you are under the age of 50

WHERE IS THE STUDY GOING TO TAKE PLACE AND HOW LONG WILL IT LAST?
The study activities may happen at rural clinics near your home. They may also happen at the Center for Memory & Aging at the University of New Mexico. You may spend up to 10 hours plus travel time in this study each year. You may do part or all of the study at one of these locations. The location of your visits will depend on your preferences, location availability and other reasons. The study activities may be spread out over two or more visits.

WHAT WILL YOU BE ASKED TO DO?
If you agree to be in this study, you will be asked to read and sign this Consent Form. You will be asked questions to make sure you understand. After you sign the Consent Form, the following things will occur:

Medical Information and Exam: Study staff will ask you questions about your personal and medical information. You will meet with a doctor or medical provider. They will complete an exam and ask you questions. During the exam they will measure your height, weight, blood pressure, pulse, and nervous system function (neurological exam). They will ask about your medications and other medical information.

Questionnaires: You will be asked to answer some questions about your feelings (such as "Do you feel sad?"). You will also be asked questions about how you go about completing everyday tasks and about your day-to-day experiences.

Study Partner: We ask all participants to identify a study partner, if available. A study partner might be a spouse, family member, friend or neighbor who you see often. A study partner will be asked to give information about themselves, such as their age and how they know you. They will answer questions about your memory, behavior, and daily functioning. You are still able to participate if you don't have a study partner.

Cognitive Testing: You will take some tests that ask you to remember things and show your thinking abilities. This could take 1 hour. During some of the tests, your responses will be audio recorded to help us score correctly. Any recordings not already deleted will be deleted at study closure.

MRI (Optional): A radiographic picture of your brain will be taken. This is called a Magnetic Resonance Image, or MRI. This is done by the Mind Research Network which is an organization partnered with New Mexico ADRC. This is optional.

Before this brain picture is taken, you will be asked questions about your medical history. This is because there may be reasons that you will not be able to get an MRI. For example, since the MRI is a strong magnet, brain pictures cannot be taken when there are certain pieces of metal in our body. An example of metal in your body could be bullets, shrapnel, or some other metal fragment. Another example would be certain types of medical devices that may not be allowed. You will be asked to take off your jewelry. It will be kept safely in a locker. You may be asked to change into medical scrubs for the MRI. This is just in case you have metal in your clothing, like a zipper.

The MRI acts like a camera that we use to take pictures of your brain. If the MRI results are unclear, you may be asked to have an additional brain picture taken. To have your brain picture taken, you will lie down on a table. You will then be placed into a long donut-shaped magnet. During the study, you will hear loud tapping and knocking noises coming from the magnet. You will be given headphones to wear to protect your hearing. You may also listen to music. You may feel warm during this process. In order to obtain good brain pictures, you should not move during the scan. You will be able to talk with research workers during breaks or in case of emergency by pressing a call button. This picture session will last for 1 hour but may be 1-2 hours including the before and after time needed.

This brain picture is being taken to answer research questions, not to look at your brain for medical reasons. This brain picture is not a replacement for a doctor-ordered picture. This research picture might not show problems that may be picked up by a doctor-ordered picture. However, a neuroradiologist - a doctor with experience reading MRI scans - will read all research MRI scans. You will receive a copy of these results. They will be discussed with you by one of the study doctors.

Blood Tests: A sample of blood (approximately 4tsp (20ml)) will be taken with a needle. The blood will be stored at the Center for Memory & Aging at UNM in Albuquerque. Tests will be done on markers of brain health. Once all studies are done, your blood will be committed to a final resting place, according to your preferences or your community's rules. No blood will be shared with any outside groups and no genetic testing will be done.

Data Sharing and Management: Brain pictures and other information you provide will be shared with National Institute on Aging and other National Institutes of Health data repositories indefinitely. The data cannot be linked to you because it is assigned a code number. Your name, medical record number, or other information that could easily identify you will not be stored with your data. These data may be shared by National Institute on Aging to approved researchers. The key that links your identifying information to the code number will be stored securely in a separate file. The key will not be shared with anyone who is not on the study team.

Yearly Follow-up Visits: We would like to see all participants each year until you are no longer able or willing to come back. At every yearly visit, you will answer the same questions and complete the same activities you did the first time. The number of years we are able to follow participants will depend on renewed funding, but may be 10 or more years. If you are no longer able to come in for visits, we may try to contact you or your study partner by phone to check-in to see how you are doing. If you do not want to be contacted, please let us know at 505-272-3315 or in writing at:

Center for Memory & Aging

Albuquerque, NM 87131-0001

WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS AND DISCOMFORTS?
Clinical Examination: Medical questions sometimes may cause worry. Cognitive Testing and Questionnaires: The thinking tests and the questions you are asked may lead to tiredness or emotions. You may become aware of some thinking problems you are having. A question about how you feel may make you think about some hard things in your life. Audio recordings of your responses may involve a slightly greater risk of loss of privacy. We will only identify the recording by study ID. These recordings will be deleted when the study is closed
if not already deleted.

Blood Drawing: The risks of drawing your blood include soreness, bruising, pain, infection, possible fainting, and bleeding.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI): The risks of having your brain picture taken with the MRI are small. Radio and magnetic waves associated with MRI examinations are not associated with any known bad effects

The most common problem is claustrophobia in about 5% of people. Claustrophobia is an uncomfortable feeling of anxiety or discomfort about being inside the donut-shaped magnet tube. If this happens, you will come out of the scanner quickly and uncomfortable feelings will resolve.

The machine also makes loud noises such as knocking or buzzing during the scan. You will be offered headphones to protect your hearing and make it more comfortable. You can also listen to music.

Sometimes, people are dizzy when first standing up after lying down for over an hour. This passes fast. Research workers will have you sit for a brief period until you are ready to walk out of the scan area.

Problems can occur as it relates to metal objects (such as certain heart pacemakers) in or around your body. The strong magnet can affect these metal objects. If these are present, you will not be able to do the scan. Research workers will assure there are no reasons for concern. They will ask questions before your scan. There might be a risk that a metal object will be released in the room during the study. That object could injure you. Every effort will be made to assure that no metal objects are present during your study

No long-term harmful effects from taking brain pictures are known. However, we don't yet know whether or not taking these brain pictures impacts the early development of unborn babies. Because of that, people who are pregnant should not go in the MRI

Rarely, large tattoos can heat up during an MRI scan and cause skin irritation like a sunburn, so the research worker will want to see any tattoos you have prior to the scan

There is a risk of seeing something on the brain pictures that may or may not be important. If this happens, a study investigator will talk to you about this in person or over the telephone. This may result in worry and need for additional pictures. You may get a recommendation for a clinical brain picture to be taken. This would be not be paid for by the study. If the radiology report becomes part of your personal medical record, it may or may not have an effect on getting health insurance or life insurance in the future. If you agree, the results can be sent to your medical provider. Your doctor may complete additional tests to confirm the findings

Other Risks: As with all studies, there is always a possibility of a breach of privacy. There is always a chance that any medical activity can cause harm. The research activities in this study are no different.

There may also be side effects or risks to study participation that are not known at this time.

Title of Research Study: Alzeihmer's Disease Research Center - Positron Emission Tomography (PET) Imaging INFORMED CONSET OPTIONAL

Consent to Participate in Research

Supported By: The National Institute on Aging

This addendum consent is for an optional procedure. All the consent language in the main consent still applies.

You are being invited to participate in an optional procedure as part of your enrollment in the NM Alzheimer's Disease Research Center. If you are interested in participating in this optional study, you will complete 2 PET scans. These scans will measure the Amyloid Beta and the Tau in your brain.

Why are researchers doing this study?
This study seeks to identify the several possible causes of dementia that may be present in an individual, and that may be contributing to a person's current or future cognitive symptoms. We invite you to take part in this optional procedure because you are already a volunteer participant in the New Mexico Alzheimer's Disease Research Center. The data and information from this study will become part of your main Alzheimer's Disease Research Center study record. The data from this study will also be shared broadly with the scientific community to enable discoveries on the dataset that you are helping to create. Information that can identify you will not be included with any of the shared
research data. The same data sharing from the main consent applies to this sub study.

ARE THERE REASONS WHY YOU WOULD NOT QUALIFY FOR THIS STUDY?
You cannot participate if you are pregnant or breastfeeding. You will need to be able to lay still for the
PET scans and be willing to complete 2 PET scans. There are no additional reasons you cannot get a PET
scan.


WHERE IS THE STUDY GOING TO TAKE PLACE AND HOW LONG WILL IT LAST?
The PET scans may be conducted in different locations for different participants depending on your situation and the study's situation. It is possible you may complete the 2 PET scans in different locations.

  1. PET scans may be conducted at UNM hospital
  2. PET scans may be conducted at Banner Health in Phoenix. If this is the case, your travel expenses would be paid for by the research study.

The 2 PET scans will be completed within 3 months of each other, but may be completed as close as a day apart. If you would like to receive the results of your research, this will occur at a later date.

Detailed Information:
The rest of this document includes detailed information about this study (in addition to the information listed above).

WHAT WILL YOU BE ASKED TO DO?
If you take part in this research, you will be asked to undergo two brain PET imaging tests, detailed below.
These will need to be done on different days. The tests are well accepted standard procedures and all
use FDA approved drugs. There are no treatments in this study.

Summary of procedures that will be done:

  • Eligibility review and consent
  • Amyloid PET scan with one of the following tracers: florbetapir (Amyvid) or flutemetamol (Vizamyl)
  • Tau PET scan with the following tracers: flortaucipir (Tauvid)

PET Scans: You will have 2 different types of brain positron emission tomography (PET) scans to evaluate for signal consistent with amyloid plaques and tau tangles. These will be completed on a PET/CT scanner, simply refer to these scanners as a "PET scanner''. For each scan you will also have a CT scan that is part of the PET scan procedure. 

Before participating in PET scans, participants of childbearing potential will have a pregnancy test. 

Each PET scan uses a small amount radiation. This radiation is in addition to what you may get as part of your regular medical care. Everyone receives low levels of natural radiation, called background radiation. This comes from outer space and from rocks and minerals in the soil. Natural background radiation is greater at higher altitudes. The average yearly background radiation in the United States is 3 mSv. The amount of additional radiation you will get will be about 12 millisievert per PET scan. This is equal to about 4 years' worth of natural radiation. This is less than the maximum amount a person working with radiation is allowed to get in 1 year.

The radioactive material, called a radiotracer, is injected through a vein in your arm. After the radiotracer is injected, you will rest for a period of time while the radiotracer circulates into the brain. Additional time is needed between the injection and the scan. During the time between the injection and the scan, you will be able to relax in a waiting room. This waiting time may be up to 80 minutes. You can bring a snack and activity to do while you wait. During the scan, try to hold your head as still as possible. The technician will help you find a comfortable position for this and may use cushions or tape to hold your head in a comfortable position. The actual PET scans may last up to 20 minutes.

Amyloid PET: This type of PET scan measures the amount of any amyloid plaques present in your brain. Amyloid is a protein associated with Alzheimer's Disease.
Tau PET: This type of PET scan measures the amount of any abnormal tau protein accumulations in your brain. Abnormal tau proteins form the neurofibrillary tangles of Alzheimer's Disease.

After each PET scan please drink extra fluids and empty your bladder to help clear the compounds from your system. The compounds will clear your system within a few hours. The total time needed for each scan may be 2-3 hours.

WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS AND DISCOMFORTS?
Risks of PET imaging: The primary risk of PET is radiation exposure. The amount of radiation involved in this research is small, but may slightly increase your risk of getting cancer later in life. Scientists are not certain about the actual cancer risk at these low doses, and there may be no risk at all. The radiation amount, including exposure from head CT scan, is well below the annual limit set by the federal government for research subjects. To minimize radiation exposure, the study uses the lowest possible dose of radioactivity necessary to obtain a quality image.

Radioactive ligands cannot be given to women who are pregnant. If you are a woman of childbearing
age, you will get a pregnancy test, paid for by the study, before the PET scan. PET scans require intravenous (IV) injection of radioactive chemicals (tracers). There is a slight risk of discomfort, bruising, fainting or infection with placement or removal of the IV needle. There is risk of mild to moderate pain (burning or stinging sensation) during injection of the tracer. This typically goes
away within a few seconds. Thrombophebitis, or vein inflammation that can cause blood clots in the vein, is a known risk for any IV needle placement.

The radiotracers used in this research have FDA approval for use for the same FDA approved reason. This study is not testing these tracers is an experimental way. The tracers that may be used in this study are Flutemetamol (Vizamyl) and Florbetapir (Amyvid) which are used for amyloid PET, and Flortaucipir (Tauvid) which is used for tau PET.

The FDA-approved tracers have undergone extensive development and rigorous safety testing. The tracers have been used extensively worldwide in research with no related serious adverse effects. 

Uncommon side effects of Amyvid include headache (1.8%), muscle or joint pain (0.7%), nausea (0.7%), fatigue (0.5%), and injection site reactions (0.5%).

Uncommon Side effects of Vizamyl include flushing (2%), headache (1%), increased blood pressure (2%), nausea (1%), and dizziness (1%).

Uncommon Side effects of Tauvid include headache (≤ 0.5%), injection site pain (≤ 0.5%), and increased blood pressure (≤ 0.5%).

Serious but rare side allergic reactions to any of the tracers can cause rash or redness include hives, swelling of the face, lips, tongue or throat, and difficulty breathing.

WILL YOU BENEFIT FROM TAKING PART IN THIS STUDY?
Being in this study will not help you directly. Your participation in the study may benefit other people in the future by helping us learn more about how to detect and diagnose causes of cognitive impairment and dementia. 

If you choose to learn your research PET results you may benefit because this information could be helpful as you make future healthcare decisions.

WILL YOU BE GIVEN INDIVIDUAL RESULTS FROM THE RESEARCH TESTS?
You may have the option to learn if your scans show elevated or not elevated levels of amyloid and tau in your brain. Before deciding if you want to learn your PET results, we will explain the process and discuss the risks and benefits with you. We will also describe the possible test results and their meaning.

The results from these research scans are not equivalent to a clinical diagnosis.

WHAT WILL IT COST YOU TO PARTICIPATE?
There will be no cost to participate.

CAN YOU CHOOSE TO WITHDRAW FROM THE STUDY EARLY?
You can choose to leave the study at any time. You will not be treated differently if you decide to stop taking
part in the study. If you stop being in the research, already collected data or samples may not be removed from
the study database. Data distributed to approved researchers may still be used for research studies.

WILL I BE PAID FOR PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?
You will be compensated $200 for each PET scan that you complete. It is expected that participants will
receive 2 PET scans as part of this study for a total of $400.

You will receive a merchandise cards for compensation for your participation. If you earn $600 or more by participating in research, it is potentially reportable for tax purposes. If you travel to Phoenix for one or more PET scans, your travel will be covered by the research study.

What other choices do I have besides taking part in this research?
This is an observational study and the alternative is to not participate.

Do I have to be in the study?
No, you do not have to be in this study. Taking part in research is voluntary. If you decide not to be in this study, you will not lose medical care or any legal rights. You can ask all the questions you want before you decide.

Will information from this study go in my medical record?
If you have any of your PET scans at UNMH, a medical record may be created for you if you do not already have one. Your medical record might say that you participated in this study, and a copy of this consent and authorization form might go in your medical record. The radiological reads for each of the PET scans as well as the CT scan will be in your UNMH medical record.

If you have a PET scan at Banner, the results of those tests will not be in you UNMH medical record. It is still possible that these results could end up in your medical record if you give these results to one of your UNMH doctors.

WHAT IF NEW INFORMATION IS LEARNED DURING THE STUDY THAT MIGHT AFFECT YOUR DECISION TO PARTICIPATE?
You will be informed if the investigators learn new information that could change your mind about staying in the study. You may be asked to sign a new informed consent form if the information is provided to you after you have joined the study.

WHAT ELSE DO YOU NEED TO KNOW?
If you participate in this sub study, you will be one of about 120 people to do so as part of the New Mexico Alzheimer's Disease Research Center.

Title of Research Study: Alzheimer's Desease Research Center - Lumbar Punture INFORMED CONSENT OPTIONAL

Principal Investigator: Gary Rosenberg, MD

Consent to Participate in Research

Supported By: The National Institute on Aging

This addendum consent is for an optional procedure. All the consent language in the main consent still
applies.

Procedures

Lumbar Puncture: A lumbar puncture is sometimes called a spinal tap. Some people cannot have this test because, for example, they are on blood thinning medication. A medical doctor will perform the test, involving removal of spinal fluid through a needle. You will be asked to lay down or sit leaning over an exam table for this procedure. This test will take about 60 minutes. The spinal fluid will have labels bearing your study number and the date it was taken. Some of the spinal fluid will be sent to a central laboratory (Tricore, Albuquerque, NM) for routine tests and some of the fluid will be kept in Dr. Rosenberg's laboratory. All samples that have not been previously used or destroyed will be destroyed at the study closure.

The lumbar puncture will be done when you are fasting (have not eaten in the last 8-12 hours), so is typically scheduled for morning. If you have the optional Lumbar puncture, your blood will be drawn and will be used to compare the chemicals found in your spinal fluid to those found in your blood.

Venipuncture/Blood Tests: At the same visit as the Lumbar Puncture, a blood sample of about 2-4 teaspoons will be taken with a needle from a vein in your arm. This blood sample will be used to compare to the spinal fluid. Some of the blood sample will be sent to a central laboratory with the spinal fluid.

Biological Sample and Data Sharing: The blood and spinal fluid samples will have labels bearing your study number and the date it was taken. Some samples will be stored in Dr. Rosenberg's laboratory in freezers for future analysis.

The biological samples may be provided to researchers at academic institutions, hospitals, and private companies. Specimens collected from you for this research may be used to develop products which could be sold in the future. The researchers do not plan to share any profits or losses from the sale of those products with you.

Any samples that remain in Dr. Rosenberg's laboratory that have not been previously used or destroyed will be destroyed at the study closure.

Risks

Venipuncture/Blood Drawing: The risks of drawing your blood include soreness, bruising, pain,
infection, possible fainting, and bleeding.

Lumbar Puncture: The lumbar puncture may cause pain at the site where the needle goes in and the spinal fluid is taken. This will be minimized by giving the skin an injection of medicine to numb the area. Lidocaine will be used to numb your back. Please inform the investigator if you are allergic to Lidocaine. There is a small risk of infection or bleeding. Risks of bleeding will be minimized by not doing a lumbar puncture on people who take blood thinners or have other problems forming blood clots. If you are on warfarin, dabigatran, rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban or other Low Molecular Weight heparins you will not be able to receive a lumbar puncture. If you have had lower back surgery or have significant back issues, you may not be a candidate for having the optional lumbar puncture.

An infection could spread throughout the nervous system. Risk of infection will be minimized by cleaning your skin very carefully. Although these are serious problems of lumbar puncture they are so rare that there is no information about how often they occur.

After the lumbar puncture you may get a headache. About a third of adults report a headache after an LP. This headache is different from other headaches because it will go away when you lay down and come back when sitting or standing. If you have this kind of headache and it does not go away after 48 hours, call one of the research staff. To minimize the risk of a headache, the doctor will use a small needle and may prescribe bed rest for one or more hours after the procedure. If a headache occurs, it is usually mild and can be controlled by bed rest, drinking lots of fluids and a pain pill, such as acetaminophen. Rarely, the headache is severe and may require additional treatment with a "blood patch." In this procedure, a small amount of your own blood is injected into the lumbar puncture site. This procedure is generally effective in stopping the headache.

To minimize these risks, the lumbar puncture procedure will be performed by a medical professional specifically trained to do this procedure.

The amount of spinal fluid that is drawn is minimal (4 teaspoons). The spinal fluid that we take is replaced by your body in a matter of hours.

WILL YOU BE GIVEN INDIVIDUAL RESULTS FROM THE RESEARCH TESTS?
Specifically, for the tests of Spinal Fluid, you will receive the results of tests that are standard. Some of the tests are research-based and cannot be used to make decisions about standard medical care. As this is a research study and not standard medical tests, any results may take significantly longer than it would in a regular medical setting and you will not be given the results of research performed on the biological samples that are not standardized tests.

The sample and/or information that you are donating will no longer belong to you. The research may lead to new medical knowledge, tests, treatments, or products. These products could have some financial value. There are no plans to provide financial payment to you or your relatives should this occur.

WILL I BE PAID FOR PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?
You will be compensated $100 for the Lumbar Puncture. You will receive a merchandise cards for compensation for your participation. If you earn $600 or more by participating in research, it is potentially reportable for tax purposes.

Instructions for measuring waist and hip circumference in adults

Waist circumference should be measured at the midpoint between the lower margin of the last palpable rib and the top of the iliac crest, using a stretch resistant tape. Hip circumference should be measured around the widest portion of the buttocks, with the tape parallel to the floor.


For both measurements: Participant should stand with feet close together, arms at the side and body weight evenly distributed, and should wear little clothing. The participant should be relaxed, and the measurements should be taken at the end of a normal expiration. Each measurement should be taken twice and entered here. If the difference between the two measurements exceeds 0.5 inches, the two measurements should be repeated.

Source: Waist circumference and waist-hip ratio: report of a WHO expert consultation, Geneva, 8-11 December 2008.

Source: NHLBI Obesity Education Initiative, nhlbi.nih.gov

 Steps for proper blood pressure measurement 
STEP 1 - Properly prepare the participant:
 
•   Have the participant relax, sitting in a chair (feet on floor, back supported) for >5 minutes
•   The participant should avoid caffeine, exercise, and smoking for at least 30 minutes before measurement.
•   Ensure that participant has emptied his/her bladder.
•   Neither the participant nor the observer should talk during the rest period or during the measurement.
•   Remove all clothing covering the location of cuff placement.
•   Measurements made while the participant is sitting or lying on an examining table do not fulfill these criteria.
STEP 2 - Use proper technique for BP measurements
 
•   Use a BP measurement device that has been validated and ensure that the device is calibrated periodically.
•   Support the participant's arm (e.g., have it resting on a desk).
•   Position the middle of the cuff on the participant's upper arm at the level of the right atrium (midpoint of the sternum).
•   Use the correct cuff size, such that the bladder encircles 80% of the arm, and note if a larger- or smaller-than-normal cuff size is used.
•   Either the stethoscope diaphragm or bell may be used for auscultatory readings.
STEP 3 - Take proper measurements
 
•   Take two BP readings in both arms.
•   Separate the second set of measurements from the first by one minute.
•   For auscultatory determinations, use a palpated estimate of radial pulse obliteration pressure to estimate SBP.
     Inflate the cuff 20-30 mm Hg above this level for an auscultatory determination of the BP level.
•   For auscultatory readings, deflate the cuff pressure 2 mm Hg per second, and listen for Korotkoff sounds.
STEP 4 - Properly document accurate BP readings
 
•   Record SBP and DBP. If using the auscultatory technique, record SBP and DBP as onset of the first Korotkoff
     sound and disappearance of all Korotkoff sounds, respectively, using the nearest even number. 
•   Record the two readings of SBP and DBP in the left arm, and the two readings of SBP and DBP in the right arm.
STEP 5 - Give BP readings and interpretation to the participants
 
•   It is recommended to provide participants with the SBP/DBP readings both orally, and in writing.
Source:  Checklist for accurate measurement of BP adapted from AHA Guidelines, Whelton PK et al., Hypertension. 2018; 71: e13-e11.

INSTRUCTIONS: This form is to be completed by the clinician or other trained health professional, based on co-participant report and behavioral and neurological exam of the participant. In the extremely rare instances when no co-participant is available, the clinician or other trained health professional must complete this form using all other available information and his/her best clinical judgment. Score only as decline from previous level due to cognitive loss, not impairment due to other factors, such as physical disability. For information on the required online CDR training, see the UDS Coding Guidebook for Form B4.

INSTRUCTIONS: This form should be completed by a clinician with experience in performing a comprehensive neurologic examination, assessing the presence/absence of neurological signs, and rating the degree of any abnormalities. Additionally, the clinician should have experience in completing each of the assessment measures associated with the gateway questions if any key neurologic findings are present.  For additional clarification and examples, see UDS Coding Guidebook for Form B8.

Field Label: Other:

Section 2D - Additional Measures

There are several additional clinical measures to consider for completion depending on the findings and the suspicion of the clinical syndrome; these include, but are not limited to, the following:
a ) If there are any features of a movement disorder (e.g., bradykinesia, tremor, rigidity, postural instability, etc.): Consider completing Form B3 UPDRS, or the MDS-UPDRS

b ) If there are any features of ALS (e.g., upper motor neuron dysfunction and/or lower motor neuron dysfunction): Consider completing the ALSFRS-R

c ) If there are any features of PSP- Richardson's syndrome (e.g., parkinsonism, postural instability, supranuclear gaze palsy, etc.): Consider completing the PSPRS

d ) If there are any features of corticobasal syndrome (e.g., limb rigidity, limb apraxia, myoclonus, dystonia, corticol sensory loss, alien limb phenomenon, etc.): Consider completing the PSPRS and/or the CBFS

e ) If there are any features of complex visual processing dysfunction (e.g. hemineglect, visual agnosia, simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, ocular apraxia, apraxia of eyelid opening, etc.): Consider completing a standardized measure assessing PCA

f ) If there are any features of aphasia or apraxia of speech (e.g., NIH Stroke Scale, Progressive Aphasia Severity Scale, Western Aphasia Battery, etc.): Consider completing a standardized measure assessing speech and language

g ) If there are clinical and/or imaging findings suggesting a vascular contribution to the clinical presentation: Consider completing NIH Stroke Scale, Hachinski Ischemic Scale, etc.

Section 2E - Glossary of Abbreviations

ALS = Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
ALSFRS-R = Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis Functional Rating Scale-Revised
CBS = Corticobasal Syndrome
CBFS = Cortical Basal ganglia Functional Scale
LMN = Lower Motor Neuron
MDS-UPDRS = Movement Disorders Society - Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale
PCA = Posterior Cortical Atrophy
PLS = Primary Lateral Sclerosis
PSP = Progressive Supranuclear Palsy
PSPRS = Progressive Supranuclear Palsy Rating Scale
SMA = Spinal Muscular Atrophy
UMN = Upper Motor Neuron
UPDRS = Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale

The next four questions ask about your gender identity, sex assigned at birth, sexual orientation, and intersex status. This information will be used to help us improve health, well-being, and quality of care. By gender identity, we mean the inner sense that you have of yourself as being a man, woman, or a different gender. Gender identity can be different from your sex assigned at birth or your sexual orientation, and it can change over time.

INSTRUCTIONS: The following questions are designed to gather information on your current and past life experience that we think may be important for brain health. There are no right or wrong answers, and you do not have to answer any question that makes you feel uncomfortable. If the question does not apply to your experience, feel free to check Prefer not to answer. You should fill out this form on your own, without help from your co-participant or study partner.

INSTRUCTIONS: This form is to be completed by a clinician with experience in evaluating participants with neurological and psychiatric  diagnoses. Estimates are allowed if exact birth year or age at death is unknown. For additional clarification and examples, see the UDS  Coding Guidebook for Form A3.

Section 1 - Biological parents

For any parent with a neurological or psychiatric diagnosis, the entire column must be filled out.


If the clinician cannot determine the primary neurological/psychiatric diagnosis after reviewing all available evidence, select Specific Diagnosis Unknown in the Primary diagnosis row, and skip the subsequent questions in the remaining rows. For a parent with no neurological or psychiatric diagnosis, select No known neurological/psychiatric diagnosis in the Primary diagnosis row, and then skip the subsequent questions in the remaining rows. For a parent with a primary diagnosis but no secondary diagnosis, select No secondary diagnosis in the Secondary diagnosis row.

YEAR OF BIRTH FOR FULL SIBLINGS & BIOLOGICAL CHILDREN: If birth year is unknown, please provide an approximate year on UDS Initial Visit Form A3 and UDS Follow-up Visit Form A3 so that the sibling or child with unknown birth year ends up in correct birth order relative to the other siblings/children.

Example: A subject is the oldest of three children. The subject was born in 1940 and the middle sibling in 1943; the youngest sibling's birth year is unknown. An approximate birth year of 1944 or later should be assigned to the youngest sibling.

Use that same birth year on FTLD Module Form A3a, if applicable, and across all UDS visits so that any new information on a particular sibling or child can be linked to previously submitted information. If it is impossible for the subject and co- participant to estimate the birth year, enter 9999=Unknown.

3. How many biological children does the participant have? {kids:icons} 

{a3_section39no66}

For any biological child with a neurological or psychiatric diagnosis, the entire column must be filled out.

If the clinician cannot determine the primary neurological/psychiatric diagnosis after reviewing all available evidence, enter 99 = Unknown in the Primary diagnosis column, and skip the subsequent questions in the row. For a biological child with no neurological or psychiatric diagnosis, enter 00 = No known neurological/psychiatric diagnosis in the Primary diagnosis column, and then skip the subsequent questions in the row. For a biological child with a primary diagnosis but no secondary diagnosis, enter 88 = No secondary diagnosis in the Secondary diagnosis column.

INSTRUCTIONS: This form is to be completed by the clinician or ADRC staff based on the medical history interview with the participant and co-participant, as well as review of any medical records that are available. Any conditions identified during the visit should be included on the form. For additional clarification and examples, see UDS Coding Guidebook for Form A5/D2. Select only one answer per question, unless otherwise stated.

Cigarette smoking

1b. Total years smoked

INSTRUCTIONS: This form should be used to record treatments expected to significantly impact Alzheimer's disease and related dementias (ADRD) biomarkers, whether a disease-modifying treatment that is FDA-approved for ADRD and received as part of clinical care or an investigational treatment received as part of a clinical trial.  For treatments received as part of clinical care, only those that are FDA-approved for disease-modification of ADRD should be included on this form.  If the participant is receiving one of these treatments as part of their clinical care at the time of clinical assessment (e.g., they are receiving lecanemab infusions), the treatment should be included on both this form and the A4 Medication form. Participation in any ADRD drug trial over an individual's lifetime should be included. If available, the ClinicalTrials.gov identifier should be entered into the "specific treatment and/or trial" cell. Information on the type of treatment can be found via ClinicalTrials.gov and is summarized in "Alzheimer's disease drug development pipeline." 1This form should be completed by the clinician based on participant interview and/or co-participant report. For additional clarification and examples, see UDS Coding Guidebook for Form A4a. Select only one answer per question, unless otherwise stated.

Dementia criteria

Requirement #1:

Participant has cognitive decline or behavioral (neuropsychiatric) symptoms that meet all of the following criteria:

 Requirement #2:

Participant must have impairment in one* or more of the following domains:
  • Interfere with ability to function as before at work or at usual activities
  • Represent a decline from previous levels of functioning
  • Are not explained by delirium or major psychiatric disorder
  • Include cognitive impairment detected and diagnosed through a combination of: 1) history-taking; 2) objective assessment (bedside or neuropsychological testing)
  • Impaired ability to acquire and remember new information
  • Impaired reasoning and handling of complex tasks, poor judgment
  • Impaired visuospatial abilities
  • Impaired language functions
  • Changes in personality, behavior, or comportment

*In the event of single-domain impairment (e.g., language in PPA, behavior in bvFTD, visiospatial in posterior cortical atrophy, etc.), the participant must not fulfill criteria for MCI.

MCI core clinical criteria

4. {loc_mcicrit}

4. MCI core clinical criteria
Choices, Calculations, OR Slider Labels: 1, Clinical concern about decline in cognition compared to participant's prior level of lifelong or usual cognitive function (e.g., based on input from participant, co-participant, and/or the clinician's judgment, CDR SB 0.5+, etc.)|2, Impairment in one or more cognitive domains, compared to participant's estimated prior level of lifelong or usual cognitive function, or supported by objective longitudinal neuropsychological evidence of decline|3, Largely preserved functional independence OR functional dependence that is not related to cognitive decline (e.g., based on clinical judgment)

Field Label: 4a1. MCI criteria - Clinical concern about decline in cognition compared to participant's prior level of lifelong or usual cognitive function

4a2. MCI criteria - Impairment in one or more cognitive domains, compared to participant's estimated prior level of lifelong or usual cognitive function, or supported by objective longitudinal neuropsychological evidence of decline
4a3. MCI criteria - Largely preserved functional independence OR functional dependence that is not related to cognitive decline
4b. Does the participant meet criteria for MCI (amnestic or non-amnestic)?

Cognitively impaired, not MCI/dementia

The purpose of the "Cognitively impaired, not MCI/dementia" category is to capture those individuals with evidence of cognitive impairment or decline who do not meet formal MCI criteria.

Check all applicable criteria for cognitively impaired, not MCI/dementia in Q5, using any relevant data.

Affected Domains - Dementia and MCI

Choose domains that are impaired at the current visit based on clinical judgment informed by clinical history and neuropsychological testing. Select one or more as Impaired; all others will default to unimpaired in the NACC database.

Mild Behavioral Impairment (MBI) core clinical criteria
  • Participant, co-participant, or clinician identifies a change in the participant's affect, motivation, thought content, behavior, or personality that is clearly different from their usual affect, motivation, thought content, behavior, or personality
  • Symptoms have been present at least intermittently for the last six months or longer
  • Late onset (i.e., age > ~50, unless early onset neurodegenerative syndrome is suspected)
  • Not explained by delirium, other psychiatric disorder by DSM criteria (including recent onset, longstanding or recurrence of longstanding disorder)
  • Symptoms interfere with at least one of these: work, interpersonal relationships, social activities
  • Largely preserved independence in other functional abilities (no change from prior manner/level of functioning, or uses minimal aids or assistance)

Section 2 - Clinical syndrome
The purpose of Section 2 is to assign a predominant clinical syndrome to participants with dementia and, when appropriate, MCI or MBI, using all available clinical, exam, and neuropsychiatric data.  This should be done using clinical information and cognitive/neuropsychological testing, ideally without reference to biomarker data (which is incorporated into the Etiological Diagnoses section in Form D1b). This is not always possible and thus Q9 allows centers to record when biomarker data is known and may have influenced the clinical diagnosis.
